mnemonics.title: RST for Equivalence Relation
mnemonics.content: Remember RST: Reflexive (R) - relates to self; Symmetric (S) - two-way street; Transitive (T) - chain reaction. All three needed for EQUIVALENCE!

mnemonics.title: Partition Properties
mnemonics.content: NPE: Non-empty (each block has elements), Pairwise-disjoint (no overlap), Exhaustively covers (union = whole set).

mnemonics.title: Class Equality Test
mnemonics.content: To check if classes are equal: If a~b then [a]=[b]; If a≁b then [a]∩[b]=∅. Either equal OR disjoint!

acronyms.usage: Three properties required for an equivalence relation
Three conditions for a valid partition of a set
Set of equivalence classes under congruence modulo n; has n elements [0] to [n-1]

visualTips.visualization: Imagine set A as a big circle containing smaller non-overlapping circles (partitions). Each small circle is an equivalence class - all points inside are equivalent to each other.
Draw A as a rectangle with disjoint oval regions inside representing equivalence classes. No overlap, complete coverage - like a jigsaw puzzle.
For mod 3, mark integers on line. Draw different colored arcs connecting: {..., -6, -3, 0, 3, 6...}, {..., -5, -2, 1, 4...}, {..., -4, -1, 2, 5...}. Three distinct infinite sets.

analogies.analogy: Like students in a school grouped by grade level - all 10th graders are 'equivalent' to each other in that sense, forming one group (class). Each student belongs to exactly one grade.
Like dividing a pizza into slices - each slice is non-empty, slices don't overlap (disjoint), and together they make the whole pizza (exhaustive).
Like telling time on a 12-hour clock - 13:00 and 1:00 are 'equivalent' (same position), showing only n distinct positions exist (clock hours).
